eli3: Model degradation is the gradual decline in model performance over time. Unlike sudden failures, it's a slow erosion — death by a thousand cuts as the world changes around your model.
Causes: data drift, concept drift, feedback loops (model's own predictions affect future data), system changes (feature pipeline bugs, upstream data changes), and simple staleness (world evolved).
Prevention requires continuous monitoring, automated retraining triggers, and graceful degradation strategies. Don't wait for users to complain; monitor metrics proactively.

whenWhy.title: Production ML maintenance
whenWhy.body: Models not 'set and forget'. Netflix, Google report models degrade weeks to months after deployment. Without monitoring, gradual degradation goes unnoticed. Continuous evaluation catches issues early.

whenWhy.title: Regulatory compliance
whenWhy.body: Finance (SR 11-7): model risk management requires ongoing monitoring. Healthcare: model drift can affect patient outcomes, requires validation. Credit: fair lending laws require ongoing fairness monitoring.

whenWhy.title: Business impact prevention
whenWhy.body: Recommendation model degrading → lower engagement → lost revenue. Fraud model degrading → more fraud losses. Catch degradation before business metrics suffer. Prevention cheaper than cure.

whenWhy.title: Root cause analysis
whenWhy.body: Degradation diagnosis: is it data drift, concept drift, software bug, or external factor? Monitoring differentiates. Data drift in specific features suggests pipeline issue. Sudden drop suggests bug. Gradual suggests true drift.

deepDiveIntro: Degradation timeline: Initial deployment (high performance) → honeymoon period (stable) → gradual decline (data/concept drift) → critical threshold breached → user impact → manual intervention. Goal: catch decline before critical threshold.

Monitoring pyramid: (1) Infrastructure: latency, error rate, throughput. (2) Data: feature distributions, schema validation, drift detection. (3) Model: prediction distributions, confidence scores. (4) Business: conversion rate, fraud caught, recommendations clicked. (5) Ground truth: accuracy, AUC, F1 (when labels available). Lower levels provide early warning for upper levels.

deepDive.heading: Monitoring Metrics
deepDive.body: Input: feature statistics (mean, variance, null rate), schema validation, drift scores (PSI, KS). Output: prediction distribution, confidence scores, calibration error. Ground truth: accuracy, precision, recall, AUC, business KPIs. System: latency p50/p99, error rate, resource utilization. Coverage: per-segment (region, device type) to catch subset degradation.

deepDive.heading: Alerting Strategies
deepDive.body: Threshold-based: alert when metric < threshold (simple, many false positives). Statistical: alert when p-value < 0.05 comparing to baseline (statistical significance). Change point detection: CUSUM, Bayesian online changepoint detection. Composite: multiple indicators must fire (reduce false alarms). Severity: warning (investigate), critical (rollback).

deepDive.heading: Graceful Degradation
deepDive.body: When model degrades, have fallback strategies: (1) Simpler model: rule-based backup if ML model confidence low. (2) Human-in-the-loop: route uncertain predictions to humans. (3) Conservative predictions: favor 'safe' class (e.g., don't approve suspicious loans). (4) Default values: use population averages. (5) Feature flags: disable model instantly without code deploy.

deepDive.heading: Automated Remediation
deepDive.body: Self-healing systems: (1) Auto-rollback: deploy previous model version if error rate spikes. (2) Auto-retraining: trigger training pipeline on drift detection. (3) Circuit breaker: stop serving predictions if too many errors. (4) Load shedding: drop non-critical traffic during degradation. (5) Alert escalation: page on-call engineer if auto-remediation fails.

deepDive.heading: Countermeasures
deepDive.body: Champion/challenger: always have candidate model being evaluated. Ensemble: combine multiple models for robustness. Online learning: adapt continuously to changes. Monitoring-driven development: build in observability from start. Regular retraining: schedule refreshes before degradation significant.

takeaways: Model degradation = gradual performance decline after deployment.
Causes: drift, feedback loops, system changes, staleness.
Monitor: infrastructure → data → model → business metrics.
Alerting: threshold, statistical, change point, composite.
Remediation: graceful degradation, auto-rollback, circuit breakers.

edgeCases.q: How do you set degradation thresholds?
edgeCases.a: Business-driven: what accuracy loss costs $X? Statistical: 3-sigma from baseline. Empirical: historical degradation patterns. Conservative in high-stakes (medical, safety), lenient in low-stakes (recommendations). Dynamic: adjust based on seasonality (expect degradation during holidays). Multi-tier: warning at 2% drop, critical at 5% drop, emergency at 10% drop.

edgeCases.q: What if labels are extremely delayed (months)?
edgeCases.a: Use leading indicators: (1) Proxy metrics (upstream events correlated with target). (2) Feature drift as early warning. (3) Human evaluation: label sample manually. (4) Prediction monitoring: distribution shifts. (5) A/B test: keep old model running on small traffic as control, compare to new model. Imperfect but better than flying blind for months.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle catastrophic failure vs gradual degradation?
edgeCases.a: Different alerts and responses. Catastrophic (sudden drop to 0% accuracy): immediate rollback, page on-call, post-mortem. Gradual (slow decline over weeks): schedule retraining, investigate root cause, plan model refresh. Detection: rate-of-change threshold for catastrophic, cumulative deviation for gradual. Different playbooks for each.

sources.label: Sculley et al. — Machine Learning: The High Interest Credit Card of Technical Debt
Breck et al. — What's Your ML Test Score?
